data.title: Celcuity's High-Stakes Oncology Bet Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Celcuity (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:CELC) has spent almost all its resources developing gedatolisib, a pan-PI3K/mTOR inhibitor for advanced breast cancer. The company remains under the radar, with a valuation reflecting skepticism rather than potential despite being designated as a Breakthrough Therapy and demonstrating strong early efficacy data. With pivotal Phase 3 trials underway and a differentiated IV delivery mechanism to help alleviate toxicity concerns, the market may be misjudging Celcuity's risk-reward profile. Speculative plays have been punished in the current biotech environment, but I believe Celcuity's focused strategy may make it an overlooked winner later in 2025 and beyond.

Financials

The financials paint a picture of a company spending heavily to expand its pipeline - expected for a clinical-stage biotech company. Operational expenses rose to $30.1 million in Q3 2024 from $18.9 million in the prior year. This was largely due to an increase in R&D expenses to $27.6 million as the company ramped up clinical trial activity. That sort of spending can be a double-edged sword. On one hand, it indicates meaningful progress in late-stage trials - especially the VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2 studies. But it also shows how heavily Celcuity relies on its cash position to fund operations - with no revenue stream to offset the burn.

As a buffer, cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments stood at $264.1 million at the end of Q3 2024. With $20.6 million of operating cash burn for the quarter, Celcuity theoretically has about 13 quarters of runway before it needs more funding if burn rates remain steady. But this timeline may shift with trial expenses, potential regulatory milestones, or strategic shifts. With strong results from VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2, the company may be able to secure non-dilutive funding or a partnership rather than raising capital through equity - a trap often faced by small biotech firms.

The impact of a prolonged bear market for speculative biotechs is also not fully appreciated. Those that rely entirely on investor sentiment to fund their next phase of growth are often punished preemptively despite having healthy cash reserves. I believe this is representative of Celcuity's case. Due to this emotional reaction, the stock price does not currently capture all the strategic flexibility this balance sheet affords, especially when rivals developing PI3K inhibitors have had trouble due to safety issues and mixed efficacy. If Celcuity proves safer, it might be in a unique position to profit from an area of oncology that has seen several setbacks.

Clinical Pipeline

Even though Celcuity is focused on a single drug, its pipeline has depth. Gedatolib is its lead and only clinical asset developed for HR+ / HER2- advanced breast cancer, as well as additional exploratory work in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Its strategy is based on a simple, underappreciated premise: by simultaneously inhibiting PI3K and mTOR it may avoid some of the traps seen with other drugs in this space. Toxicity, including liver problems and severe hyperglycemia, has traditionally limited the use of PI3K inhibitors like alpelisib. In its intravenous formulation, Celcuity may have avoided first-pass metabolism in the liver.

VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2 trials will determine if gedatolisib can capture meaningful market share. For patients progressing after CDK4/6 inhibitor treatment, VIKTORIA-1 is evaluating the drug together with fulvestrant with or without palbociclib. It is a very competitive setting, but the scarcity of compelling second-line options makes this a commercially viable option if gedatolisib shows durable responses. Probably the bigger bet is VIKTORIA-2, which is a first-line study using Gedatolisib in combination with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. If it succeeds, it could position the drug as an early-line treatment in combination with HR +/HER2- breast cancer, expanding the market even further.

I also believe that Celcuity's relatively small but important foray into prostate cancer is overlooked. An overlooked value driver could be the ongoing Phase 1b/2 trial in patients progressing on androgen receptor inhibitors but not chemotherapy. In prostate cancer, combination approaches have emerged, mainly targeting PI3K / AKT / mTOR pathway. Prostate cancer drugs with even modest efficacy may have very broad commercial prospects. The market generally shuns single-asset biotech companies with thin pipelines but, as Celcuity has demonstrated, one well-executed bet may be enough. The main question is not whether the drug works - early data suggest it does - but whether the company can demonstrate its differentiation in a way that regulators and prescribers see value in.

Targeting PI3K and mTOR

Complex signaling networks drive breast cancer, especially the HR +/HER2- subtype, to adapt to targeted treatments and survive. The most critical are PI3K and mTOR, which both regulate cell growth, metabolism, and survival. Mutations in PIK3CA, the key subunit of PI3K, are present in about 40% of HR+/HER2- breast cancers and are a well-known target. Past attempts to drug this pathway have had toxic results, especially with oral PI3K inhibitors that induce severe metabolic disturbances by interfering with insulin signaling. This has created a cautious regulatory environment, with the FDA increasing requirements for PI3K-targeted agents after several safety failures.

Celcuity's approach is unique because gedatolisib is not only a PI3K inhibitor but also an mTORC1/2 inhibitor, which may target resistance mechanisms that arise when only one component of the pathway is targeted. PI3K and mTOR are in the same signaling cascade, and inhibition of only PI3K can induce mTOR compensatory activation, reducing long-term efficacy of treatment. By targeting both, gedatolisib may provide more sustained tumor growth suppression in cancers that have developed resistance to standard endocrine therapy and CDK4/6 inhibitors. This dual mechanism is not only theoretically advantageous - early clinical data suggest that patients with and without PIK3CA mutations respond to the drug, suggesting that it may work independently of mutational status, unlike alpelisib, which is approved only for PIK3CA-mutant tumors.

Prostate cancer is an interesting new frontier for gedatolisib beyond breast cancer. The PI3K / AKT / mTOR axis also drives disease progression in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, especially in tumors resistant to androgen receptor inhibitors such as enzalutamide and abiraterone. The loss of the PI3K signaling-regulating tumor suppressor PTEN is observed in about 40% of prostate cancers, leading to hyperactivation of this pathway. This suggests that blocking PI3K and mTOR simultaneously may benefit tumors that have exhausted standard therapy options. Especially compelling is the fact that prostate cancer therapies historically have lacked novelty, which leaves a space in the market for novel targeted therapies. If it can show activity in this space, gedatolisib could present Celcurity with a secondary market opportunity that very few are paying attention to.

Encouraging Survival Data Strengthens Gedatolisib’s Case

In its latest press release, Celcuity reported overall survival data from its Phase 1b trial of gedatolisib in patients with HR +/HER2- advanced breast cancer that may be able to compete with existing therapies. Patients receiving gedatolisib in combination with palbociclib and endocrine therapy had a median OS of 77.3 months compared to 33.9 months for patients previously treated with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. These numbers are comparable to historical data for standard-of-care regimens and support Celcuity's Phase 3 trials.

Those findings are especially compelling because they align with Celcuity's strategy of blocking several resistance pathways at once. PI3K/mTOR inhibition in combination with CDK4/6 blockade and endocrine therapy appears to prolong survival beyond that observed with these treatments alone. Though there are always caveats to cross-trial comparisons, the extended OS in treatment-naive patients may suggest that gedatolisib may be effective earlier in settings important to the VIKTORIA-2 trial. We can expect weaker OS in pretreated patients who have progressed on standard therapy already; however, survival over 30 months is still noteworthy even in this population.

The significance of these findings remains to be fully appreciated by the market, probably due to lingering skepticism about PI3K inhibitors as a class. Still, Celcuity is positioning itself as the developer of a differentiated safety profile and the potential for broad efficacy beyond PIK3CA-mutated tumors. If the Phase 3 data repeats the early success, investor sentiment could change quickly.

Risks That Could Derail Celcuity’s Bet

The most immediate concern would be whether targeting PI3K and mTOR together will yield a meaningful clinical advantage without creating new toxicity problems. Whilst gedatolisib's intravenous delivery may limit liver-related side effects with oral PI3K inhibitors, its long-term safety profile is uncertain. The class of PI3K inhibitors has been characterized by problems beyond hepatotoxicity, including immune suppression and metabolic dysregulation. Even if delivered differently, patients may still experience serious adverse effects that prevent widespread adoption.

Another question is whether dual inhibition of PI3K and mTOR will be sufficient to prevent resistance mechanisms indefinitely. Tumors have a tendency to adapt - sometimes bypassing one signaling cascade by upregulating another. For example, the AKT pathway between PI3K and mTOR may still permit cancer cells to escape inhibition when not adequately suppressed. The question remains: is blocking both PI3K and mTOR sufficient to prevent compensatory resistance, or will tumors find other routes? Academic work has pointed out that targeting this pathway is complex and that even in preclinical models, combined inhibition induces resistance due to crosstalk with other survival pathways such as MAPK and JAK/STAT.

In this space in particular, I think competition is another underappreciated risk. Although CDK4 / 6 inhibitors are still the mainstays of HR + / HER2- breast cancer therapy, new-generation SERDs and AKT inhibitors are showing promise in clinical trials. Any of these emerging treatments with better efficacy or cleaner safety profiles might limit gedatolisib's market penetration even if it is approved. Investors should also consider the financial risk - Celcuity has a healthy cash position, but biotech burn rates can skyrocket. If trial timelines extend or unexpected costs occur, they may to another round of fundraising being required, causing dilution.

Valuation and Market Potential

Despite its massive spending on pre-commercial R&D, conventional valuation metrics fail to capture Celcuity's potential. Rather, the company is valued on its market potential for gedatolisib, particularly in the HR +/HER2- advanced breast cancer. In the U.S., the HR +/HER2- subtypes will account for about 70% of the approximately 310,720 new invasive breast cancer cases, or about 217,504 patients annually by 2024.

Concerning advanced stages, 6-10% of breast cancers are metastatic at diagnosis - de novo metastatic breast cancer. Averaging that across all of the HR + / HER2- population suggests an estimated 13,070 to 21,750 annual new de novo metastatic HR + / HER2- breast cancer cases.

The treatment costs for advanced breast cancer therapies are also quite high. For instance, Palbociclib (Ibrance) costs about $118,200 per year for patients. Assuming a similar price of gedatolisib, I would estimate that de novo metastatic patients could represent a US market size of approximately $1.55 billion to $2.5 billion annually.

This estimate excludes patients progressing to metastatic disease following first treatment or patients with recurrent disease, which may further expand the addressable market. Furthermore, with proven efficacy in other cancers such as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, gedatolisib may have expanded market potential.

Future Outlook

While the financials give Celcuity enough cash runway to get the pivotal trials through, the company largely relies on one asset. Due to this, any setback could be far greater than expected. Its valuation argument is more about market size than traditional revenue multiples - a size that would easily justify a much higher valuation if gedatolisib succeeds.

The market has not yet embraced gedatolisib's potential differentiation, likely due to skepticism about the PI3K pathway and the broader biotech downturn dampening sentiment for speculative names. Should Celcuity pull through on its upcoming readouts, current levels may prove to be a mispricing in retrospect. But I would emphasize that it is also an all-or-nothing proposition - the company is making a single bet that must pay off for shareholders to see the upside.

data.title: 10 Ideal 'Safer' January Dividend Buys, Out Of 40 Choices From Readers
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail, or comment to the author has been fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction. Examples include the reader who missed my “safer” dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios. The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha.

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers.

Still, every month, readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

This year, SA has listed all the postings on my Dogcatcher investing group by date. So to find the summary and reference guide to each dog of the week portfolio, look at:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024, and will post its last return on October 3, 2025.

Last winter, a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M.

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher.

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However, I find the number of charts is a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago, when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are complaints of too many words for little information.

A July 2024 prize comment was:

“anybody able to plough through this cruff and figuire which two stocks are buys in accordance with his title? these articles are borderline autistic.

Additionally, I do not think INTC is overbought. Its 14-day RSI of 48.11 indicates that the company is trading in a technically neutral state. The RSI is a stock indicator that ranges from 0 to 100, with overbought stocks being above 70 and oversold stocks being below 30.

—weitermachen 8/15/24.

I replied:

“@weitermachen Thanks for your comments! Just to clarify and crystallize the two suggested ideal dogs noted my July Dogs of the Dow article, they are the two Dow Index stocks showing dividends (from $1k invested) exceeding single share price: Verizon Communications, and Dow Inc.

As for the “overbought” three, I'm using an unsophisticated measure (unlike RSI) to back up my assertion. “Unbuyable” stocks is perhaps a better term for Goldman, JPMorgan, & Intel, whose dividend yields exceed their free-cash-flow yields. I have since employed terms ”cash rich” or “cash poor” to describe the status of ideal and not so ideal stocks.

Regards, FA.

This past month, I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating. I now describe the “safer” stocks as “cash rich” and the stocks whose dividends from $1K in vested do not equal their single share price as “cash poor.”

In June 2022 high-yields were alleged, by one reader, to be sure signs of Rogueishness. Not true; high-yields are sure signs of high-yield, how long the elevated yield lasts, depends on share-price and corporate directors. High-Beta is the most accurate measure of Rogueishness.

In February 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly paying dividend stocks:

“You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk.”

[See a summary of ten fair-priced December ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and five “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my “ideal” stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equals or exceeds share price. A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, recently spun off from Liberty media. A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Currently priced at $27.11, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 3.08%! A little over $1k investment now buys 37 shares, and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) of nearly 1.47 times the share price. Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 25 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in Nvidia Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly, nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (Recently $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $3,215.43.)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: when the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 20.84% To 56.81% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By January 2025

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this January 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-28 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to January 2026 were:

TORM PLC (TRMD) was projected to net $568.13, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 77% less than the market as a whole.

Frontline PLC (FRO) was projected to net $566.68, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 99% less than the market as a whole.

Petrobras (PBR) was projected to net $544.34, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% over the market as a whole.

Park Hotels (PK) was projected to net $535.03, based on the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 100% over the market as a whole.

Golden Ocean Group (GOGL) was projected to net $441.44, based on the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 17% greater than the market as a whole.

Merck & Co (MRK) was projected to net $431.27 based on the median of target prices estimated by 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $279.46 based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 36% under the market as a whole.

Dow Inc (DOW) was projected to net $241.62, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 18% over the market as a whole.

Verizon (VZ) was projected to net $219.81, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 55% under the market as a whole.

Conagra Brands (CAG) was projected to net $208.35, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 72% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 40.36% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 22% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the “dog” moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as “dogs.” More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, “underdogs.”

The January 2025 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The January 2025 ReFa/Ro By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top January ReFa/Ro Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.66% To 38.81% Per YCharts

Top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors and also included 1 Closed End Investment Co (CEIC).

Three energy representatives placed first, second and ninth, Petrobras [1], TORM PLC [2], and Frontline PLC [9].

A lone real estate entity placed third, Park Hotels [3].

Three financial representatives placed fourth, sixth and eighth, Invesco Mortgage (IVR) [4], ARMOUR Residential (ARR) [6], and FS KKR Capital (FSK) [8].

A lone CEIC, placed fifth, Carlyle Credit (CCIF) [5]. Another loner, an industrial, Golden Ocean placed seventh (7), and, finally, one consumer staples member placed tenth. Altria Group (MO) [10], to complete the January ReFa/Ro Dogs by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten January ReFa/Ro dogs by dividend and price as of market close 2/17/2025.

This month, none of the top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs show a less than ideal status because all ten prices are lower than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by all ten of the highest yield ReFa/Ro Dogs.

All ten lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding a single share prices.

34 ReFa/Ro Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 2/17/25

The lower chart reveals 34 ReFa/Ro Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the January ReFa/Ro was Petrobras at $2.58 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive was Johnson & Johnson at $31.48 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Seventeen of Forty ReFa/Ro Dogs Show No Dividends or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Fifteen ReFa/Ro so infected are joined by two non-dividend payers: Petrobras, TORM PLC, Park Hotels, Invesco Mortgage, Carlyle Credit, Frontline, Dow, LyondellBasell, Pfizer, Enterprise Products, Canadian Utilities, Main Street Capital, Kenvue, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund, Reeves Utility Index Fund, are the fifteen. The two non-dividend payers (discovered by readers) were Banco BBVA Argentina, and Banco Macro. These seventeen are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive over-reach, and tariffs, too.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 16.3% To 49.97% Top Ten ReFa/Ro Upsides To January 2026; (32) Four -1.36% to -18.01% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a “market-sentiment” gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield “dog” metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 11.39% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 January ReFa/Ro Dogs As of 2/17/ 25

Ten-top ReFa/Ro dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs, selected 2/17/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors plus one Closed End Investment Corporation.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield ReFa/Ro Dogs (34) To Deliver 36.53% Vs. (35) 32.79% Net Gains by All Ten Come January 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 11.39% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, TORM PLC, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 56.81%.

The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for January were: Carlyle Credit; Invesco Mortgage; Golden Ocean; Park Hotels; Petrobras, with prices ranging from $7.88 to $14.32.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of January were: Frontline; ARMOUR Residential; TORM; FS KKR Capital; Altria, with prices ranging from $17.67 to $53.29.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' “basic method” for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of “market sentiment” gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the markets dies, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements will gain momentum into 2025 except the few tech stocks. Look for at least half of the ten ReFa/Ro Dogs to become fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy 10 of 27 “Safer” January ReFa/Ro Dogs

January 2025, of the 27 Ideal Re/Fa/Ro were tagged “safer” for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. The ten “Ideal” candidates from the “safe” group showed dividend yields from $1,000 invested exceeding their single share prices.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Re/Fa/Ro Updates, out here, in public, away from the fire-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of “dividends” from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your “Safer” ReFa/Ro dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

data.title: Building A $100,000 Dividend Portfolio: Enhancing SCHD's Income With High-Yield Stocks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Here on Seeking Alpha, I have already highlighted in previous articles my conviction about the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) as an excellent investment option for long-term investors that want to blend dividend income and dividend growth to steadily build wealth over time.

However, with a current Dividend Yield [TTM] of 3.56%, the ETF offers investors limited potential for the generation of dividend income. For this reason, in this article, I will show you how to build a $100,000 dividend portfolio with the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF as a core-element while enhancing the ETF with February’s top 10 high dividend yield companies and five additional high dividend yield companies that can help to increase the ETF’s potential for income generation via dividends. Simultaneously, these high dividend yield companies can increase the portfolio’s level of diversification while reducing its volatility.

The objective of the portfolio I am presenting today is to significantly increase the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF’s capacity to generate dividend income. Such a portfolio is particularly attractive for investors looking to generate a significant amount of extra income via dividends, superior to the dividend income you can reach when only investing in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF. Within the past weeks, several of my followers have asked me to publish an article about such a dividend portfolio.

This dividend portfolio not only delivers investors strong potential for the generation of dividend income (its Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] stands at 6.37%), but also elevated potential for dividend growth (reflected in its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 7.58%).

The companies’ strong dividend growth potential is underlined by 13 of the 15 exhibiting a positive EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR], which underscores their capacity to constantly increase earnings per share, allowing them to increase dividends over the long term.

The attractive Valuation of the companies is reflected in the portfolio’s Weighted Average P/E Ratio of 14.62, which is significantly below the P/E Ratio of the S&P 500 (20.95). This comparison suggests that investors can invest with a margin of safety when following such an investment approach.

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™

The graphic below illustrates that the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF has performed in line with the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:VOO) during the past 10 years. While the S&P 500’s Total Return has been 187.47%, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF has shown a Total Return of 181.12%.

Risk Analysis of This Dividend Portfolio

Risk Analysis of the 15 Individual Positions of This Dividend Portfolio

Growth Outlook: EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR]

In my opinion, the EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR] is an important financial metric, since it indicates a company’s capacity to increase its earning per share over the long term. It is also an indicator of the company’s dividend growth potential and the sustainability of its dividend. Given the long-term investment focus I am following with my investment approach, this metric is particularly important.

Thirteen of the 15 individually selected companies I have included in this dividend portfolio exhibit a positive EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR], serving as an indicator of their capacity to enhance profits per share and of the sustainability of their dividend and increased dividend growth potential.

The companies with the highest EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR] are PNC Financial Services Group (20.05%), Zurich Insurance Group (17.28%), Iberdrola (8.37%), Restaurant Brands International (7.77%), Chevron (6.89%), PepsiCo (5.09%), and Phillips 66 (5.00%).

Free Cash Flow Yield [TTM] (And Alternatively the FFO Yield [TTM] for REITs)

The chart below illustrates the Free Cash Flow Yield [TTM] of the companies that are part of this dividend portfolio.

The companies with the highest Free Cash Flow Yield [TTM] are Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. – Petrobras (with a Free Cash Flow Yield [TTM] of 32.11%), Verizon (11.25%), Altria (9.29%), Zurich Insurance Group (8.38%), Restaurant Brands International (6.19%), and Chevron (5.47%).

For the REITs, such as NNN Reit and VICI Properties, the FFO Yield [TTM] is illustrated since it is the more accurate metric for REITs. VICI Properties presently has a FFO Yield [TTM] of 8.97% and NNN REIT of 8.67%.

The companies’ elevated Free Cash Flow Yields are additional indicators of their attractive Valuations, which is another indicator that investors can invest with a margin of safety when following such an approach.

It is worth mentioning that Ares Capital, MAIN Street Capital, and PNC Financial Services Group are not illustrated in this graphic since the Free Cash Flow Yield [TTM] is less meaningful to them.

The Dividend Yield [TTM]

The portfolio’s elevated capacity for the production of dividend income is underscored by the attractive Dividend Yield of the ETFs, the Income Fund and the individually selected companies.

With a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 19.49% Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. – Petrobras offers the highest income, followed by the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (Dividend Yield [TTM] of 13.53%), and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (with a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 9.68%). This makes them important strategic additions within this dividend portfolio.

Ares Capital (Dividend Yield [TTM] of 8.31%), Altria (7.61%), Verizon (6.69%) and Enterprise Products Partners (6.36%) also pay investors attractive amounts of dividends, which reflects their importance for this portfolio.

Consecutive Years of Dividend Growth

The portfolio’s capacity to provide investors with dividend growth potential is not only underlined by the companies’ elevated EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rates [3-5Y CAGR], but also by their strong track record of dividend enhancements.

Eight of the 15 individually selected companies have shown more than 10 consecutive years of dividend growth.

The companies with the most consecutive years of dividend growth are Altria (55 consecutive years of dividend growth), PepsiCo (52), Chevron (37), NNN REIT (34), Enterprise Products Partners (26), Verizon (20), PNC Financial Services (14), and Phillips 66 (12).

Valuation: P/E [FWD] Ratios

The companies I have selected for this dividend portfolio have an attractive Valuation: none of the 15 presently has a P/E [FWD] Ratio above 20, and 12 exhibit a P/E [FWD] Ratio below 15.

The portfolio’s Weighted Average P/E [FWD] Ratio stands at 14.62 while the S&P 500’s P/E [FWD] Ratio is presently at 20.95, further underscoring the companies’ attractive Valuation.

The companies with the lowest Valuations are Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. – Petrobras (P/E [FWD] Ratio of 6.03), Zurich Insurance Group (7.79), Verizon (8.68), Altria (10.03), Ares Capital (10.49), VICI Properties (11.20), Enterprise Products Partners (11.48).

The Projection of the Portfolio’s Dividend and Yield on Cost

As I have already mentioned, the portfolio’s Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] stands at 6.37% while its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] lies at 7.58%.

Assuming that you would today invest the amount of $100,000 into such a dividend portfolio, you could potentially generate a dividend income before taxes of $6,370 in 2025, $13,226 in 2035 and $27,464 in 2045. This is under the assumption that the portfolio’s Average Dividend Growth Rate for the next 20 years would be in line with its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 7.58%. This projection underscores the portfolio’s suitability for long-term investors.

Risk Analysis of the Portfolio’s Company-Specific Concentration Risk When Distributing SCHD, JEPQ and PDI Across Their Companies

Given the careful selection process of this dividend portfolio, no single company accounts for more than 5% of the overall portfolio, even when distributing SCHD, JEPQ, and PDI across the respective companies they are invested in.

When allocating the ETFs (SCHD and JEPQ) and the fund (PDI) across their respective companies, Chevron (with a proportion of 4.39%), PepsiCo (4.38%), Verizon (4.36%), Altria (4.21%), VICI Properties (4.04%) and NNN REIT (4.00%) account for the largest proportions.

PNC Financial Services Group, Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. – Petrobras, Main Street Capital, Enterprise Products Partners, and Ares Capital each account for 3% of the overall portfolio.

Zurich Insurance Group, Restaurant Brands International, Phillips 66 and Iberdrola make up 2.50% each of the overall portfolio.

All remaining companies are not direct investments, instead they are indirect investments via one of the ETFs (SCHD and JEPQ) or the fund (PDI), and represent no more than 2% of the overall portfolio. This means that their impact on the portfolio’s performance is reduced when compared to the companies which hold a larger proportion.

Risk Analysis of the Portfolio’s Sector-Specific Concentration Risk When Distributing SCHD, JEPQ and PDI Across Their Sectors

The graphic below illustrates the sector distribution of this dividend portfolio when allocating SCHD, JEPQ and PDI across their companies and sectors they are invested in compared to the sector allocation when only investing in SCHD.

It can be noted that this dividend portfolio offers investors an increased allocation to the Real Estate Sector (9.04% compared to 0% when exclusively investing in SCHD), and to the Utilities Sector (2.95% compared to 0.03%), suggesting a broader sector diversification and reduced level of risk when compared to only investing in SCHD.

The Principal Risks when Implementing such a Dividend Portfolio

Even though I have carefully selected the ETFs, funds and companies that are part of this dividend portfolio, investing in such a portfolio still comes attached to several risks, which I will further explain in this section.

The main risk factor when following such an investment approach is the possible underperformance of the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund.

Even though they can be attractive strategic elements to help us increase the portfolio’s capacity to generate dividend income, I believe they are likely to underperform when investing over the long term when compared to investing in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF or the S&P 500.

For this reason, I suggest allocating a significantly higher proportion to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF when compared to the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund. This strategic approach increases the likelihood of achieving a higher Total Return.

Additionally, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund’s limited dividend growth potential can be highlighted. This is another reason why I suggest allocating a higher proportion to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

I suggest not allocating more than 10% to each the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund to decrease the risk level of your investment portfolio and to position it for dividend growth potential and capital appreciation, alongside its potential to generate dividend income.

Conclusion

The objective of today’s article was to show you how to build a $100,000 dividend portfolio with the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF as the core holding while including high dividend yield companies to enhance the ETFs ability to provide dividend income.

The portfolio not only has a strong capacity to produce dividend income (underlined by its Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] of 6.37%), but also offers dividend growth potential (which is reflected in its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 7.58%).

I believe that such a portfolio can be suitable for long-term investors that want to produce a significant amount of dividend income with the potential to raise this amount on an annual basis.

Given the dividend growth potential of the selected companies (which is reflected in their elevated EPS FWD Long Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR]), and their presently attractive Valuations (the portfolio’s Weighted Average P/E Ratio is 14.62 compared to the S&P 500’s P/E [FWD] Ratio of 20.95), I believe that such a portfolio also has potential for capital appreciation.

Author’s Note: Thank you for reading! Which high dividend yield companies are the core-elements of your dividend portfolio? Which companies do you think would best fit the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF to increase its capacity for the generation of dividend income?

data.title: RTX Provides An Attractive Opportunity Amidst Defense Turmoil
data.description:
I covered RTX (NYSE:RTX) in January with a buy rating. Since then, the stock has declined more than 4% and is now trading 6.5% off its highest stock price over the past 52 weeks. The reason for that is that aerospace and defense stocks have been trading lower on the prospects of cuts to defense budgets, and RTX has been no exception. In this report, I explain why I believe the drop in stock prices might be overdone.

Revenue Growth Is Driven By Commercial Aerospace, Not Defense

One thing to keep in mind is that growth is driven by the commercial aerospace growth cycle and not so much by defense. Last year, commercial aerospace sales grew by 11.8% adjusted for the revenue charge related to the metal powder issues for the geared turbofan, while U.S. government revenues increased only 2% and total military revenues seemingly declined 1.3%. President Trump has been talking about the ability to cut defense budgets in half. I don’t believe that this is the solution at a time when adversaries are increasing their weapon system capabilities and arsenals and the U.S. and its allies have to come up with additional spending to keep up with the evolving threat environment. The reality is that even if China and Russia slow their defense spending, there remains a gap for the U.S. to close, particularly on hypersonics and hypersonic missile defense. Freezing budgets will not accelerate weapon system development and freeze the U.S. in a spot where it might be at a disadvantage. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth has been mild, seeking an 8% cut, but that is still substantial.

Since 2020, U.S. defense sales grew at a rate of 5.6% while commercial aerospace grew at a rate of 14.1%. If we cut U.S. defense sales by 8% from 2024 levels and allow for continued growth in line with the CAGR for commercial aerospace, there would still be 3.5% growth in revenues. Furthermore, we noted that international sales have grown at a rate of 6.7% annually, which would add another 100 basis points to the growth. Using last year’s revenue growth for the commercial aerospace segment would still provide 3% revenue growth.

What we also do see is that U.S. government revenues in 2024 represented 40% of sales. That's substantial, so a cut will hurt, but relative to total sales we have to conclude that the U.S. government is becoming a smaller part of the pie already and that also holds for its share in military revenues. I believe that while a decline in U.S. government sales will hurt, there are offsets in the form of commercial aerospace sales as well as international sales. European countries might rethink their procurement favoring European contractors where possible, but the reality is that it will still largely depend on U.S. defense contractors and a cut to the defense budget in the U.S. is not an indication that European countries can do the same as the partnership with the U.S. currently is viewed as highly uncertain.

RTX Can Lean On A $218 Billion Backlog

One thing that should be kept in mind is that RTX can lean on a $218 billion backlog, of which nearly 30% is purely defense. The total backlog covers the equivalent of nearly 2.5 years in sales, of which 25% is expected to convert into revenues in 2025. This covers 65% of the projected sales for 2025. In 2024, Raytheon (reflecting pure defense bookings) were $39.25 billion. There are no figures available from RTX filings that discuss conversion in subsequent years. However, the budget cut that's requested is for FY26 (starting Oct. 1). And that means that 2025 revenues have a few risks attached. The contracting agencies (U.S. Navy, U.S. Air Force and U.S. Army) are not the parties that have requested lower budgets, and they are actually pushed to lower the budgets while they also continuously have given signals over the years that the budgets are not matching the desired weapon system capabilities. So, it lies in line of expectation that they will use nearly every dollar in their current budgets to somewhat signal that they are already underfunded and cutting the budgets even more is undesired.

While it's not the case that the total backlog fully derisks the next 2-2.5 years, a portion of future military revenues for which there's an obligation on both sides is already in the backlog and additional contract awards this year will layer into this year’s revenues and next year’s. If you measure it against a four-year presidency, then 25% already seems to be derisked and a substantial portion of year two of the presidency will also be derisked.

Apart from that, if countries such as China and Russia and actually also Europe keep developing their weapon system capabilities, there's no way that the U.S. can just watch it happen. So, views of lower budgets being possible can still change. Furthermore, I believe that indeed with fewer dollars, more can be done. That does not necessarily eat away on the margins of defense contractors. The way contracting works now, the entire mechanism is a bureaucratic one. If DOGE is successful, a lot of unnecessary actions in the procurement process and eventually certification can be cut out without compromising on the capabilities of the end product. Currently, contracts are expensive for the sole reason that there's a lot of paperwork involved. That means that a lower budget might not necessarily reduce the margins if the budget declines are matched with prompt actions from the U.S. Government to cut down money wasted in the procurement and certification process.

A third mechanism is a simple one that we already have seen in play and that's the simple fact that defense contractors are no longer willing to burn their hands on high-risk contracts. During President Trump’s first term, many defense contractors were forced into fixed-price development contracts and those contracts have backfired. If President Trump wants to do things the same way during his second term, there's a chance that defense contractors may opt out bidding for contracts absent of any cuts in unnecessary costs in the defense procurement and development complex.

Conclusion: RTX Corporation Has An Attractive Mix Of Defense And Commercial Exposure

The reality is that defense contractors currently do not look like the most attractive investment opportunities, as defense budget cuts provide an uncertain backdrop for aerospace and defense companies. However, I do believe that companies such as RTX have a more appealing positioning within the aerospace and defense industry due to the exposure to defense as well as commercial aerospace. Furthermore, the undeniable reality is that even if Russia and China freeze budgets, they do that at a moment, they have already booked significant steps in their weapons system and deterrent capabilities, with Russia having fielded hypersonics in Ukraine and China having presented a new fighter jet. That means that the gap between the U.S. and other global powers is smaller than ever, and in terms of power projections, lower budgets eventually could diminish the position of the U.S. on the world stage, especially if other countries continue advancing.

Furthermore, we should also keep in mind that part of the sales this year and in the years thereafter are already locked in the backlog and budgets could indeed be lowered if unnecessary costs are removed from the procurement, certification and cost allocation requirements. What remains is that there indeed is significant uncertainty, but there might also be an opportunity here that will positively enhance the cost basis of defense contractors.

So, for now, I'm maintaining my buy rating for RTX stock noting that commercial aerospace holds the biggest growth prospects for the company, Raytheon is already becoming more cost-efficient, a portion of the defense contracts in the coming years is already in the backlog and cutting costs primarily by simplifying the procurement process might actually make the defense industry more agile and less cost intensive while international sales could partially offset sales losses.

data.title: Trumpflation Positions Cohen & Steers Poorly Due To Real Estate And Preferred Focus
data.description:
Cohen & Steers (NYSE:CNS) saw AUM declines towards the end of the year due to depreciation of the assets that AUM was invested in. In our previous coverage we highlighted that CNS is an exposure to long-duration assets, as CNS specialises in ETFs for REITs and to a lesser extent for preferred shares which are extremely long duration. The AUM declines reflect the possible incoming outflows that might occur following an underperformance, particularly as the macroeconomic factors around higher for longer rates seem persistent. Also, while revenues did grow on inflows YoY so did expenses and EPS stayed flat. We are still not interested in CNS and don't see how it can compete in a more binary landscape of mostly passive and some rare high performers.

Latest Earnings

The issue with these asset management companies in general is that it's becoming tougher to justify their existence with the emergence of so many savings and investing options on the market. Moreover, with extremely scaled and low cost passive investment options commodifying the market, remaining higher fee accounts are under constant pressure in what is generally seen as a losing battle. Meanwhile, inflation drives the fixed cost base, dependent significantly still on labour, up YoY and limiting the scope for profit growth even when revenue grows.

In the case of CNS, they have managed a decent revenue growth showing. Net inflows were $860 million in Q4, less than in Q3 but still positive. Though AUM fell from $91.8 billion to $85.8 billion using year end figures. Average figures over the period paint a better picture because declines were concentrated at the end of the year, which is actually, by some studies, a statistically weaker period of the year in terms of performance, with the rationale being things like loss harvesting activity that typically occurs later in the year to optimise taxes. AUM was indeed down though due to asset declines, and it also relates significantly towards the new US administration.

As an important reminder, CNS is unique in that it is highly levered to the performance of REITs, which are in turn significantly exposed to residential real estate value and real estate REITs, that don't do well in an environment where rates are high. It is the widespread and conventional thinking that Trump will lead to more inflation due to a combination of labour market pressures due to immigration policies, lower taxes (if he goes through with that) and tariffs among others. With inflation proving stubborn as it was before the handover of the presidency, the yield curve has de-inverted, the bond market therefore delivering a clear verdict that they are pricing for higher for longer. It has also translated upwards significantly over the last 6 months, reflecting the significance of the new administration. Long duration assets, such as preferred securities and also real estate, with the latter additionally affected that real estate market liquidity depends on funding costs as it is a levered purchase almost by definition, suffer when rate expectations rise.

Bottom Line

Management made reference to the fact that despite some active outperformance in preferreds, they still didn't attract significant flows there. Again, this signals that the markets are coordinating around the view that reinflation is very possible as they are eschewing poorly positioned long-duration assets. It is also clear that the Fed is taking a wait and see approach from here on out. With management expecting another 6-7% in G&A growth in 2025, the pressure is on to grow AUM, attract inflows and generate fees. Other than the constant pressure from passive which steals share way from more monetisable asset classes, AUM declines in line with the bond market move to de-invert the yield curve signal the risk of potential outflows, or at least further decelerated inflows in the Q1 as CNS' core competency areas find themselves worse positioned for the current domestic economic outlook. Outflows typically follow worse performance anyway, which is what CNS' assets delivered to clients as of the December 31 AUM figures. While the market recovered a bit in January after end of year wobbles, and the yield curve has translated down a bit in the last month, reinflation is still in play and CNS' heavy indexation to the real estate and long-duration area of the market remains a problem for us.

data.title: How Deep Is Your Non-US Love
data.description:
When it comes to the capital markets, the real action in 2025, thus far, is actually happening outside of the US.

Flows into Developed Market ("DM") are climbing, and if history is any guide this may come at the expense of the US.

Although the US keeps enjoying massive inflows for now, we could very well see a repeat of 2014-16 when DM flows skyrocketed and at the same time US flows plunged.

Last Friday, Chinese stocks reached their highest levels in at least 4 months, and it seems like a new multi-year high is a matter of when, not if.

Interestingly, while the Hang Seng Index (HSI) is surging, domestic Chinese stocks (trading on mainland China) are still somewhat struggling. Although they have picked up steam recently, they still have a lot of catching-up to do if they wish to close the divergence they've opened relative to the HSI.

But the biggest 2025 story, at least so far, is perhaps Europe.

Following two decades of underperformance and outflows, the old continent is finally showing signs of awakening.

"How deep is your love?" ask the Bee Gees, and when it comes to Europe the current-sudden love is deep, very deep, without a dip.

Last week, European stocks had their largest weekly inflow in more than two years.

Often, though not always, big inflows lead to big returns. Europe 2025 is the poster child that demonstrates a high correlation between the two (flows and returns).

Indeed, European stocks are enjoying the best start to a calendar year since (at least) the start of this millennium.

Led by China's +7.4% gain, the MSCI World Index (URTH) rallied +1.7% last week.

In terms of global major regions (China isn't included as a stand-alone country/region here), Europe is leading the way, not only over the past week (+3.4%) but also MTD (+3.8%) and YTD (+10.7%).

The longer-term trend of Europe relative to the US isn't impressive, to say the least... Nonetheless, this time (2025 YTD) is different.

One and a half months into 2025, Europe's Stoxx 600 index (STOXX) is outperforming the S&P 500 by no less than +6.65% (USD terms).

Single leading European indices have also been trouncing the S&P 500 in 2025, thus far.

Up 3.5% YTD, the S&P 500 has nothing to be ashamed of. However, Germany's DAX (+13.1%), Spain's IBEX (+12.3%) and France's CAC 40 (+11.8%) are all leaving the leading US index in the dust.

Could 2025 become one of the rare occasions that Europe outperforms the S&P 500 on a full calendar year?

Only time will tell, but so far so good for Developed Markets ex US led by Europe, as well as for Emerging Markets led by China and Brazil.

data.title: James Hardie Industries: Hard To Be Optimistic
data.description:
Today, we put James Hardie Industries plc (NYSE:JHX) in the spotlight for the first time. It is one of the largest building materials concerns most American investors have never heard of. One reason why is the company is headquartered in Europe. Roughly three-quarters of overall sales come from North America, it should be noted. The stock has been volatile in recent quarters, as can be seen in the chart below. Which way will the shares head as we get farther into 2025? An analysis follows below.

James Hardie Industries plc. is headquartered in Dublin, Ireland. The company operates out of three primary business segments: North America Fiber Cement, Asia Pacific Fiber Cement, and Europe Building Products. The company produces and distributes products like fiber cement interior linings, exterior siding products, and related accessories as well as fiber gypsum and cement-bonded boards for use in the timber frame construction, dry lining, DIY, and structural fire protection applications. The company's offerings are primarily used in residential construction, remodeling and renovation.

The stock currently trades around $32.50 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $14 billion. The company's fiscal year begins on April 1st. Management has plans to grow from having its products used in 11 million homes annually while growing its EBITDA margin by 500bps over the long term. James Hardie's primary customer base is homebuilders. The company entered into a three-year exclusive agreement with M/I Homes, Inc. (MHO) earlier this month to deliver products to use to build high-quality, climate-resilient homes. This follows similar agreements with Meritage Homes and David Weekley Homes in 2024.

Second Quarter Results

The company posted its fiscal Q2 numbers on November 13th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 36 cents a share. James Hardie's adjusted EBITDA fell eight percent from the same period a year ago to $263 million. Adjusted net income fell 12% to $157 million.

Revenues were down four percent on a year-over-year to $961 million. The good news is results in Asia and Europe were flat on a year-over-year basis.

Unfortunately, in James Hardie's largest market of North America, results were noticeably more tepid as can be seen by the synopsis below.

Management does appear confident that FY2026 will be better for the company based on guidance for the next fiscal year.

Third Quarter Results

James Hardie Industries posted its Q3 numbers after the bell on Tuesday, February 18th. The company had GAAP earnings of 36 cents a share, identical to second quarter profits. Operating margins came in at 21.6%. Adjusted EBITDA of $262 million was off seven percent from the same period a year ago. Adjusted net income $154 million fell 15%.

Revenues fell by 2.6% on a year-over-year basis to $953 million, missive expectations by some $14 million. North American results improved somewhat compared to the second quarter as sales only fell one percent year-over-year compared to five percent in the second quarter.

However, results in Asia were much worse with sales falling 12% from the same period a year ago compared to a one percent increase in the second quarter. However, this was largely due to the winding-down of the company's commercial operations in the Philippines.

Sales results from Europe were largely flat as they were in the second quarter, but EBITDA fell significantly. FY2025 guidance largely remained the same as the second quarter. Adjusted net income is projected to reach at least $635 million for FY 2025.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet

Despite a large market cap, James Hardie Industries gets little attention from Wall Street. J.P. Morgan upgraded the stock from Neutral to Outperform on November 6th. Truist Financial initiated the shares as a Buy with a $45 price target on December 10th. Bank of America maintained its Sell rating on February 7th. Truist Financial maintained its Buy rating immediately following third quarter results being posted. Those are the only analyst firm ratings I can find on JHX over the past year.

The company ended the second quarter with $368 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet and net leverage of .68X. That improved significantly in the third quarter as net leverage fell to .52X and cash on hand rose to $538 million. The company also has $600 million available on a credit revolver. James Hardie Industries purchased $75 million worth of its stock in Q2 2025 which completed a $300 million stock repurchase program. The company announced a new $300 million stock buyback authorization in November. James Hardie Industries has purchased $150 million of its own stock through the first nine months of FY2025.

Conclusion

James Hardie Industries made $1.81 a share on $3.94 billion of revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus sees profits falling on $1.47 a share on flat sales in FY2025. They project earnings to rebound to $1.74 on eight percent revenue growth in FY2026. It is important to note these consensus estimates come from only two analyst firms.

That leaves the stock trading at 18 times trailing earnings and 3.6 times revenues. The equity does not pay a dividend. That seems like a stretched valuation, given profits are projected to decline 20% in FY2025 on flat revenues. In the company's largest market of North America, existing home sales in both 2023 and 2024 in the United States came in at their lowest levels since 1995.

Home inventory is also on the rise, which doesn't bode well for future demand from home builders. Home builder profit margins are declining as home builders are forced to increase incentives like mortgage rate buy-downs and free upgrades to move their growing inventory. I went into some detail on this topic in my recent analysis around Beazer Homes (BZH).

The largest market in Europe, Germany, has had its economy contract for two years now. The rest of Europe is not much in better shape. Based on James Hardie Industries plc stock's historical price-to-sales ratio, the equity does not seem close to being in bargain territory, either. Therefore, it is difficult to be optimistic about JHX at current trading levels until the environment for the housing sector improves markedly.

data.title: Pitanium Pursues U.S. IPO On High Valuation But Low Growth
data.description:
Pitanium Is Growing Slowly For A Small Company, And The IPO Valuation Is High

Pitanium Limited (PTNM) has filed to raise approximately $7.9 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

PTNM sells skincare and haircare products in Hong Kong.

The company's small revenue base is growing only slowly, management has excessive valuation expectations and the firm presents numerous risks to investors.

Given the firm’s relatively slow growth (8.2% YOY), small revenue base, channel concentration, high valuation expectations (10x revenue vs. 2x - 4x industry) and numerous risks, my opinion on the IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Pitanium Do?

Hong Kong, PRC-based Pitanium Limited was founded to develop proprietary branded premium personal skincare and haircare products for sale in premier shopping and spa destinations in the greater Hong Kong region.

Management is headed by co-founder and Executive Chairwoman, Ms. Yuen Yi Young. She has been with the firm since its inception in 2013 and has been a co-founder of other beauty product companies with over ten years of experience in the beauty industry in various roles.

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Pitanium — high-end skincare and haircare solutions to spas

  • Big PI — aimed at retail customers in premium locations.

As of September 30, 2024, Pitanium has booked a fair market value investment of $2,113 from investors, including the co-founders of the company.

The firm sells its proprietary products to health spas as well as through six upscale retail stores in the Hong Kong region.

Pitanium also sells through its online website as it seeks to expand its product reach and revenue.

Selling and Marketing costs as a function of topline revenue have risen slightly as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

(Source — SEC.)

The Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple, i.e., how much new revenue is generated by each dollar of Selling and Marketing expense, was only 0.5x in the most recent reporting period. (Source — SEC.)

The firm has material concentration risk in its retail operations, with only six stores for its products sold through this channel. PTNM did not disclose how few spas it sells through.

There are no significant related transactions for the firm.

Market Overview For Pitanium

According to a 2024 market research report by Fitch Solutions, the market for personal care and effects in Hong Kong was expected to reach $48.2 billion by the end of 2024.

If achieved, this would have represented a year-over-year growth rate of approximately 4.6%.

The primary reasons for this growth level are a continued focus on the importance of personal appearance and increasing product selection from imported products throughout Asia.

Various competitive or other industry participants include the following:

  • The Group

  • The Preface

  • Nude Story

  • Amber Be Younique

  • Skin Needs

  • Large multinational corporations

  • Others.

Also, there is a continuing trend toward organic and environmentally friendly personal care solutions.

Online sales platforms are also seeing increasing uptake and popularity among younger demographics.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Pitanium:

Strengths

  • Proprietary brands, integration between online and offline channels.

Weaknesses

  • Market concentration in Hong Kong, no management experience in public company operation, small scale of operations

Opportunities

  • Expansion outside of Hong Kong, mobile app development, new product lines.

Threats

  • Intense market competition, uncertain Hong Kong market, regulatory risks and vulnerabilities.

Pitanium’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Moderate topline revenue

  • Increasing gross profit but lower gross margin

  • Reduced operating profit and cash flow from operations.

As of September 30, 2024, Pitanium had $2.2 million in cash and $3.1 million in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2024, of only $30,083.

IPO Details For Pitanium

Pitanium intends to raise approximately $7.9 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, offering 1.75 million shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50.

No existing shareholders have shown a desire to purchase additional shares in the IPO.

Class A shareholders will be entitled to one vote per share and Class B shareholders, the co-founders, will have twenty votes per share and will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

With a completed IPO, the firm’s enterprise value would be approximately $95.5 million, not including the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (ex-underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 7.69%, so the stock will be a ‘low float’ stock subject to potentially increased volatility in open market trading.

Volatility in low-float stocks can reach 20% to 50% in a single day.

Management said the dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $3.64, which is within the typical range of dilution at IPO.

As to lock-ups, the company and shareholders greater than 5% will not issue or sell shares for the standard 180-day period after the date of the offering.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

Approximately 15% for enhancing customer experience through launching a mobile application;

Approximately 20% for developing a new line of products solely for home treatment;

Approximately 15% for expanding its product portfolio and explore new suppliers;

Approximately 20% for further enhancing its marketing strategies; and

The balance, approximately 30%, to fund working capital and for other general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, leadership said the company was not a party to any material administrative or legal action.

The sole listed bookrunner of the IPO is Cathay Securities.

Pitanium Is Growing Slowly For A Small Firm

PTNM is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its product line expansion and related marketing efforts.

The company’s financials have generated modest topline revenue, growing gross profit but lower gross margin and decreased operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2024, was just $30,083.

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have risen a bit even as revenue has grown; its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple was only 0.5x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently intends to pay zero dividends and will likely keep any future earnings to reinvest in its growth and expansion efforts.

PTNM’s recent capital spending results show it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The market opportunity for personal care products in Hong Kong is large and expected to grow in the coming years, but it remains lower than in 2019, the year before the pandemic.

The company will have risks as a public entity, including its “foreign private issuer” and ‘emerging growth company’ status.

These designations allow management to disclose substantially less information to shareholders and Asia-based companies have typically produced only the minimum information required, leaving shareholders with little in the way of progress updates.

Also, many smaller Asian companies with low-float stocks have produced high volatility in the early days of trading, usually to the downside, quickly destroying most or all shareholder value.

Management is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of approximately 10x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of nearly 42x.

Both of these multiples are far higher than similar firms, with EV/Revenue multiples usually in the 2x - 4x range and EV/EBITDA multiples in the 10x to 15x range.

Additionally, the company is growing slowly for such a small revenue base.

Given the firm’s relatively slow growth at 8.2% YOY, undeveloped revenue base, channel concentration, high valuation expectations and numerous risks, my opinion on the IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.
